blocks.x: Chapter 8 had you running the squaring machine backward to pull a side out of an area. Now picture something different, a stadium late in the afternoon, the seats slowly filling. Above the field a long scoreboard bar is split into exactly \(100\) tiny squares, one for every hundredth of the stands, and as people arrive the squares light up one by one. Right now \(73\) of them glow. The board flashes a single word at the crowd, 73 percent full. Look at what that word is really doing. Those squares were drawn in hundredths on purpose, so a lit count of \(73\) out of the \(100\) squares is just the fraction \(\tfrac{73}{100}\) wearing a shorter name. That is the whole secret of the word. Percent comes from the Latin per centum, meaning per hundred, so \(73\%\) is literally saying \(73\) per hundred, the fraction \(73\) over \(100\). The little \(\%\) sign is not announcing some strange new kind of number. It is a costume. Underneath it a percent is an ordinary fraction with a hidden denominator of \(100\), and the sign is just standing in for that buried \(/100\). Once you can see the hidden hundred, the costume comes off easily. The same value will happily dress as the percent \(73\%\), the fraction \(\tfrac{73}{100}\), or the decimal \(0.73\), three outfits for one number. This lesson is about learning to change between those outfits without ever changing the number underneath. Three things are coming. First, a little conversion triangle that links percent, fraction, and decimal as three costumes for one value, so you can step from any corner to any other. Second, the neat reason dividing or multiplying by \(100\) does almost no work, it only slides the decimal point two places, left to take the costume off and right to put it back on. Third, the real payoff, comparing numbers that show up wearing different costumes, because once everyone is in the same outfit you can finally see who is bigger. One promise to keep us honest, this whole lesson is about what a percent is and how to move between its forms. Taking a percent of something, like how many seats \(73\%\) of a full house actually comes to, is a later story.

blocks.cap: The word percent just means per hundred, so the whole grid is split into exactly \(100\) equal squares and \(73\) of them are filled. That shaded amount is \(73\%\), which is the same as saying \(73\) out of \(100\), the fraction \(\tfrac{73}{100}\). And since a fraction over \(100\) reads straight off as a decimal, that very same shaded patch is also \(0.73\). One amount, three names, \(73\%\) and \(\tfrac{73}{100}\) and \(0.73\), and the rest of this lesson is just learning to slide between them.

blocks.x: A school mural is drawn on a perfect square wall, ten tiles across and ten tiles down, so there are exactly \(100\) tiles in all. By Friday, exactly \(41\) of those tiles have been painted. Since the whole wall is \(100\) tiles, the painted count is already a portion out of one hundred, and "per hundred" is exactly what the word percent means, so this is \(41\%\) painted. Write \(41\%\) as a fraction over \(100\) (do not simplify it yet).
hints: The word percent means per hundred, so a percent is a count sitting on top of a denominator of \(100\). What number belongs on top here, and what number belongs on the bottom?
Put the \(41\) over the \(100\). The tiles tell you the top, and per hundred tells you the bottom, so you get \(\tfrac{41}{100}\).
blocks.sol: Percent means per hundred, so \(41\%\) is read as \(41\) out of \(100\). The painted tile count gives the top number and the per hundred meaning gives the bottom number, which lands the fraction directly over \(100\). That is \(41\% = \tfrac{41}{100}\). The numerator \(41\) is prime and shares no factor with \(100\), so this fraction is already as low as it goes, but the question asked you to leave it sitting over \(100\) anyway. \(\boxed{41/100}\)

blocks.x: So here is the secret you just uncovered. The word percent means "per hundred," which means \(x\%\) is just a sneaky way of writing the fraction \(\frac{x}{100}\), with the percent sign standing in for the hidden denominator. Counting a portion out of exactly 100 hands you the percent straight away, and any other percent is the very same fraction idea with 100 quietly sitting underneath. A percent is nothing new, it is a fraction you already know how to handle.

Your phone settings screen flashes a warning. The storage drive is \(80\%\) full. That percent sign is quietly hiding a denominator, because \(80\%\) just means 80 out of every 100. Make the hidden denominator visible by writing the percent as \(\tfrac{80}{100}\), then reduce it the same way you reduced fractions back in Chapter 4. The numerator and denominator share a factor of 20. Write \(80\%\) as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: The word percent means "per hundred," so put the number over 100. That gives you \(\tfrac{80}{100}\), and now it is just a fraction waiting to be simplified.
Both 80 and 100 are divisible by 20. Divide the top by 20 and the bottom by 20 to land on the smallest equivalent fraction.
blocks.sol: Start by reading the percent literally. \(80\%\) means 80 per hundred, so write it over 100. $$80\% = \frac{80}{100}$$ Now reduce, just like any fraction. The greatest common factor of 80 and 100 is 20, so divide both the top and the bottom by 20. $$\frac{80}{100} = \frac{80 \div 20}{100 \div 20} = \frac{4}{5}$$ A quick check, \(\tfrac{4}{5}\) of the storage is the same slice as \(80\%\) of it, just written in lowest terms. So the answer is \(\boxed{4/5}\).

blocks.x: A town runs a survey and finds that \(36\%\) of households keep a vegetable garden out back. Start by writing that percent the way the word tells you to, as \(36\) per hundred, then squeeze it down. Here \(36\) and \(100\) do not share a friendly factor of \(10\), but they do both split evenly by \(4\). Write \(36\%\) as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: Percent means per hundred, so \(36\%\) is just \(\tfrac{36}{100}\). Now hunt for a common factor of the top and bottom that is bigger than \(1\).
Both \(36\) and \(100\) divide by \(4\). Compute \(36\div 4\) and \(100\div 4\), then check that the new top and bottom share no factor left.
blocks.sol: Percent means per hundred, so the survey result becomes a fraction over \(100\). $$36\% = \frac{36}{100}$$ Now reduce. The number \(10\) does not divide \(36\) evenly, but \(4\) divides both, so split the top and bottom by \(4\). $$\frac{36}{100} = \frac{36\div 4}{100\div 4} = \frac{9}{25}$$ Check that it is all the way down. The factors of \(9\) are \(1, 3, 9\), and \(25\) is built only from \(5\)s, so they share nothing but \(1\). The fraction is in lowest terms. \(\boxed{9/25}\)

blocks.x: So a percent was never a strange new creature, it was a fraction all along, just wearing a sign instead of showing its bottom number. To turn \(r\%\) into a fraction I write \(r\) over \(100\), since percent only ever meant per hundred, and then I tidy up the way I already know how, dividing the top and the bottom by their greatest common factor until nothing else divides both. The hidden denominator was \(100\) the whole time. I just dragged it out into the open and then reduced to lowest terms, exactly like any other fraction I have ever met.

blocks.cap: A percent slides straight onto a denominator of \(100\), so \(80\%\) becomes \(\tfrac{80}{100}\). From there it reduces like any other fraction, dividing top and bottom by \(20\) to land on \(\tfrac{4}{5}\). The same move turns \(36\%\) into \(\tfrac{36}{100}\), and dividing by \(4\) gives \(\tfrac{9}{25}\).

blocks.x: A tiny corner bakery had a wild Saturday. The owner posted a chalkboard sign that read "Sourdough sales today, \(250\%\) of last Saturday," which is her way of bragging that she sold two and a half times as many loaves as the week before. It feels strange at first, since a percent often sounds like it should stop at \(100\), but there is no ceiling. A percent is just a count of hundredths, so it is free to climb right past \(100\) and keep going. Write \(250\%\) as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: A percent means that many hundredths, so \(250\%\) is the same as \(\tfrac{250}{100}\). Going above \(100\) is allowed, it simply gives a fraction larger than \(1\).
Now reduce \(\tfrac{250}{100}\). Both the top and bottom share a factor of \(50\), so divide each by \(50\).
blocks.sol: A percent is a number of hundredths, so I put the percent over \(100\). $$250\% = \frac{250}{100}$$ Going past \(100\) is fine, it just means the fraction is bigger than \(1\). Now I reduce. The greatest common factor of \(250\) and \(100\) is \(50\), so I divide top and bottom by \(50\). $$\frac{250}{100} = \frac{250 \div 50}{100 \div 50} = \frac{5}{2}$$ That matches the "two and a half times" idea, since \(\tfrac{5}{2}\) is \(2.5\). So \(250\%\) in lowest terms is \(\boxed{5/2}\).

blocks.x: A rainwater tank app shows the tank sitting at a full \(100\%\). To see what that really means, write the percent the way every percent works, as that number over \(100\). So \(100\%\) becomes \(\tfrac{100}{100}\). Now simplify it and watch the fraction collapse. Write \(100\%\) as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: A percent is just a number over \(100\), so \(100\%\) means \(\tfrac{100}{100}\).
When the top and the bottom of a fraction are the same, the fraction equals a single whole number. What whole number does \(\tfrac{100}{100}\) become?
blocks.sol: Start with the meaning of percent. The word percent means per hundred, so \(100\%\) is simply \(100\) over \(100\). $$100\% = \frac{100}{100}$$ The top and the bottom are the same value, and any nonzero number divided by itself is \(1\). So the fraction does not stay a fraction at all, it reduces straight to a whole number. $$\frac{100}{100} = 1$$ A full tank, the whole thing, is exactly one whole. \(\boxed{1}\)

blocks.x: At a remote field station, a team tracks the leading edge of a glacier year after year. Over one decade the records show a change of \(-45\%\), the minus sign meaning the ice has pulled back rather than pushed forward. A percent can carry a minus sign just fine, and the "per hundred" idea does not change one bit. Negative forty five percent still means negative forty five out of one hundred. Write \(-45\%\) as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: Start from the meaning. \(-45\%\) means \(-45\) out of \(100\), so write it as \(\tfrac{-45}{100}\). The negative sign just rides along on top.
Now reduce \(\tfrac{-45}{100}\). Both \(45\) and \(100\) share a factor of \(5\), so divide each by \(5\) and keep the minus sign.
blocks.sol: The percent sign means "per hundred," so put the number over \(100\) and let the minus sign stay with it. That gives \(-45\% = \tfrac{-45}{100}\). Now simplify. Both \(45\) and \(100\) are divisible by \(5\), so divide the top and bottom by \(5\). The top becomes \(-45 \div 5 = -9\) and the bottom becomes \(100 \div 5 = 20\), so \(\tfrac{-45}{100} = \tfrac{-9}{20}\). Since \(9\) and \(20\) share no common factor bigger than \(1\), this is lowest terms. \(\boxed{-9/20}\)

blocks.x: Here is the thing worth carrying out of this lesson. A percent is never trapped between \(0\) and \(100\), because \(x\%\) is only ever \(x\) over \(100\), and the top number is free to be anything. More than one whole shows up as more than \(100\%\), so \(250\%\) is just \(\tfrac{250}{100}=\tfrac{5}{2}\), while \(100\%=\tfrac{100}{100}\) is exactly one whole. The top can shrink below \(1\), and it can even go negative, so \(100\) was never a wall, it was just the denominator hiding underneath.

A coupon at the comic shop says it takes \(38\%\) off. Before you ever touch a price tag, it helps to have that percent in a friendlier shape. Remember that \(38\%\) just means \(38\) out of \(100\), so it is really the fraction \(\tfrac{38}{100}\), which is the same as dividing \(38\) by \(100\). Rewrite \(38\%\) as a decimal.
hints: A percent is a count per hundred, so \(38\%=\tfrac{38}{100}=38\div 100\). You are not taking the percent of any price yet, just rewriting the number itself.
Dividing by \(100\) slides the decimal point two places to the left. Picture \(38\) as \(38.\) and hop that point left twice, filling the empty spot with a zero.
blocks.sol: Start from the meaning of a percent. \(38\%=\dfrac{38}{100}=38\div 100\). Dividing by \(100\) moves the decimal point two places to the left. Begin at \(38.\), hop the point left once to get \(3.8\), then left again to land at \(0.38\), writing a zero in the now empty tenths-and-ones gap. So \(38\%=\boxed{0.38}\).

blocks.x: A savings account advertises a rate of \(6\%\). The trick with a single digit like this is the sliding. Since \(6\%\) means \(\tfrac{6}{100}\), you slide the decimal point in \(6\) two places to the left, but there is only one digit to slide past, so the second place needs a zero to fill it. Write \(6\%\) as a decimal.
hints: A percent is a count out of one hundred, so \(6\%=\tfrac{6}{100}\). Dividing by \(100\) moves the point two places left.
Start at \(6.0\) and slide the point left twice. After one slide you are at \(0.6\), and the second slide needs a placeholder zero to land in the right spot.
blocks.sol: Read the percent as a fraction over one hundred, \(6\%=\tfrac{6}{100}\). Dividing by \(100\) slides the decimal point two places left, starting from \(6\) written as \(6.0\). One slide gives \(0.6\), and the second slide has no digit to pass, so a zero steps into the tenths spot to hold the place. That puts the \(6\) in the hundredths place, giving \(0.06\). You can check it back the other way, since \(0.06\) is \(\tfrac{6}{100}\), which is exactly \(6\%\). So \(6\%=\boxed{0.06}\).

blocks.cap: Dividing by \(100\) walks the decimal point two steps to the left. So \(38\%\) becomes \(0.38\), and \(6\%\) becomes \(0.06\), where a placeholder zero fills the tenths spot that the missing digit leaves empty.

blocks.x: A vibration sensor on a bridge has a tiny flaw. Its reading slowly drifts, and the spec sheet lists the drift as \(0.4\%\), less than a single full percent. To use that number in a calculation, an engineer needs it as a plain decimal. Remember that the percent sign just means "over one hundred", so \(0.4\%\) is the same as \(\tfrac{0.4}{100}\). Write \(0.4\%\) as a decimal.
hints: A percent means "divided by one hundred", so dividing by \(100\) slides the decimal point two places to the left. Start from \(0.4\) and move the point.
Moving the point in \(0.4\) two places left runs out of digits, so fill the empty spots with zeros. You get \(0.4 \to 0.04 \to 0.004\).
blocks.sol: A percent is a count out of one hundred, so \(0.4\%\) means \(\tfrac{0.4}{100}\). Dividing by \(100\) slides the decimal point two places to the left. Starting at \(0.4\), one slide gives \(0.04\) and a second slide gives \(0.004\), with zeros filling the empty places. So \(0.4\% = \boxed{0.004}\).

blocks.x: Here is the whole rule in one breath. To turn a percent into a decimal I divide by \(100\), and dividing by \(100\) just slides the point two places to the left, padding with zeros when I run out of digits. To turn a decimal back into a percent I multiply by \(100\), which slides the point two places to the right. Same two-place shift, opposite directions, so the two moves undo each other. If I ever forget which way the point goes, I lean on the meaning, since \(38\%\) means \(38\) out of \(100\), which is \(0.38\), and the shortcut always agrees with the meaning.

Your fitness app likes to keep things quiet. Instead of cheering you on with a big percentage, it just shows your goal progress for the day as the bare decimal \(0.41\). You want to brag to a friend, and "forty one percent" sounds a lot better than "zero point four one." Going from a decimal to a percent means multiplying by \(100\), which slides the point two places to the right, so \(0.41\) turns into \(41\). Write \(0.41\) as a percent. Give just the number.
hints: A percent is a count out of one hundred, so to name a decimal as a percent you multiply it by \(100\). Multiplying by \(100\) slides the decimal point two places to the right.
Slide the point in \(0.41\) two places right. It moves past the \(4\) and past the \(1\), landing after the \(1\), which leaves \(41\).
blocks.sol: A decimal becomes a percent when you multiply it by \(100\), and multiplying by \(100\) just slides the decimal point two places to the right. Start with \(0.41\). Slide the point past the \(4\) to get \(4.1\), then past the \(1\) to get \(41\). So \(0.41 \times 100 = 41\), which means \(0.41 = 41\%\). The answer is \(\boxed{41}\).

blocks.x: During a 5,000 meter race, the lap timer on the screen tracks how far the lead runner has gotten into the final lap. Right now it reads \(0.073\) of the way through. To turn a decimal into a percent, you slide the point two places to the right. With three digits after the point, two of them jump in front of the point and one digit is left behind in the tenths place, so the percent comes out as a decimal too. Write \(0.073\) as a percent. Give just the number.
hints: To go from a decimal to a percent, multiply by \(100\), which slides the decimal point two places to the right. Start by moving it from \(0.073\) one place, then a second place.
Sliding \(0.073\) two places right gives \(007.3\). Drop the leading zeros and you are left with a number that still has a digit after the point.
blocks.sol: A percent means how many hundredths, so to convert a decimal to a percent you multiply by \(100\). Multiplying by \(100\) slides the decimal point two places to the right. Starting from \(0.073\), one slide gives \(0.73\) and a second slide gives \(7.3\). Notice there were three digits after the point, so only two of them clear the point and the last one stays behind in the tenths place. That is why the percent is itself a decimal. So \(0.073 = 7.3\%\), and the number you type is \(\boxed{7.3}\).

blocks.x: The little sign \(\%\) is really just a worn-down piece of writing. Centuries ago, merchants in Italy kept their books in words, writing out per cento for "per hundred" again and again. To save ink and time, clerks trimmed it to per c., and then to a quick flourish, a small mark with a tiny circle or two tucked beside it. Across handwritten ledgers in the 1400s and 1500s the shorthand kept blurring, the little letters loosening into a stroke and a pair of small circles. Over the next couple of centuries it hardened into the slashed pair of zeros we use now, and nobody had to decide on it all at once. So the symbol is a frozen picture of its own meaning, the hundred still hiding underneath, and its cousin the per mille sign ‰ adds one more circle to mean "per thousand."

A community garden bed is split into four equal quarters, and three of them are already planted with seedlings. As a fraction, the planted part is \(\tfrac{3}{4}\). A percent is just a fraction whose denominator is hidden at \(100\), so you can read off the percent the moment the bottom number is \(100\). What is \(\tfrac{3}{4}\) written as a percent? Give just the number.
hints: You want the same fraction with a denominator of \(100\). Ask yourself what \(4\) must be multiplied by to reach \(100\), and remember to multiply the top by that same number.
Since \(4 \times 25 = 100\), multiply top and bottom by \(25\). That gives \(\tfrac{3 \times 25}{4 \times 25} = \tfrac{75}{100}\), and the numerator over \(100\) is the percent.
blocks.sol: Rewrite \(\tfrac{3}{4}\) as an equal fraction whose denominator is \(100\). Since \(4 \times 25 = 100\), multiply the top and bottom by the same \(25\). $$\frac{3}{4} = \frac{3 \times 25}{4 \times 25} = \frac{75}{100}.$$ A fraction over \(100\) is read directly as a percent, so \(\tfrac{75}{100} = 75\%\). The percent is \(\boxed{75}\).

blocks.x: A long-distance hiking trail keeps a tracker of how far you have come. Right now you are \(\tfrac{13}{20}\) of the way to the end. The tracker likes to report your progress as a percent, so it wants the same amount written with a denominator of \(100\). Since \(20\times 5=100\), multiply the top and bottom by \(5\). Write \(\tfrac{13}{20}\) as a percent. Give just the number.
hints: A percent is a count out of \(100\). Scale the fraction so its denominator becomes \(100\), then the new numerator is the percent.
Multiply top and bottom by \(5\), giving \(\tfrac{13\times 5}{20\times 5}=\tfrac{65}{100}\). The numerator over \(100\) is the percent.
blocks.sol: The denominator \(20\) reaches \(100\) when you multiply by \(5\), so scale the whole fraction by \(5\). $$\frac{13}{20}=\frac{13\times 5}{20\times 5}=\frac{65}{100}.$$ A number over \(100\) is exactly a percent, so \(\tfrac{65}{100}=65\%\). The trail is \(65\%\) finished. \(\boxed{65}\)

blocks.x: To turn a fraction into a percent, I rescale it until the bottom number becomes \(100\), and then the top number is the percent itself, because percent already means out of one hundred. A fraction like \(\tfrac{4}{5}\) lands on \(100\) cleanly whenever its denominator divides evenly into \(100\), the way \(5\) does since \(5 \times 20 = 100\), and the denominators that work this way are exactly \(2, 4, 5, 10, 20, 25, 50,\) and \(100\). And if a denominator will not divide into \(100\) so neatly, there is one move that never fails, just multiply the whole fraction by \(100\), since that is the same idea of measuring it against a hundred and it works for any fraction at all.

The top corner is labelled PERCENT and shows 75 percent. The lower left corner is labelled FRACTION and shows three quarters. The lower right corner is labelled DECIMAL and shows 0.75. Each side is a double headed arrow carrying an operation in both directions. The left side, between PERCENT and FRACTION, reads put it over 100 then simplify going down toward the fraction, and rewrite with denominator 100 going up toward the percent. The right side, between PERCENT and DECIMAL, reads divide by 100 to slide the point two places left going down toward the decimal, and multiply by 100 to slide the point two places right going up toward the percent. The bottom side, between FRACTION and DECIMAL, reads divide the top by the bottom going right toward the decimal, and write it over a power of ten then simplify going left toward the fraction. blocks.cap: Three corners, one value. Walk the left side and \(75\%\) becomes \(\tfrac{75}{100}=\tfrac{3}{4}\), then back up by rewriting with denominator 100. Walk the right side and \(75\%\) becomes \(0.75\) by sliding the point two places left, then back up by sliding it two places right. Walk the bottom and \(\tfrac{3}{4}=3\div 4=0.75\). A percent, a fraction, and a decimal are three forms of the same value, and these arrows carry you between any two.

blocks.x: A bakery is scaling up a bread recipe to \(\tfrac{8}{5}\) of the original size, since the morning crowd has been growing. The baker wants to label the new batch as a percent of the old one. A percent is just a fraction with a hidden denominator of \(100\), so to turn \(\tfrac{8}{5}\) into a percent you can rewrite it with a denominator of \(100\), or simply multiply it by \(100\). Notice that \(\tfrac{8}{5}\) is more than one whole, so the percent will land above \(100\). Write \(\tfrac{8}{5}\) as a percent. Answer with just the number.
hints: To get a denominator of \(100\), ask what turns \(5\) into \(100\). Since \(5\times 20=100\), multiply both the top and the bottom by \(20\).
Multiplying gives \(\tfrac{8\times 20}{5\times 20}=\tfrac{160}{100}\). The numerator over \(100\) is the percent.
blocks.sol: A percent is a count out of \(100\), so rewrite \(\tfrac{8}{5}\) with a denominator of \(100\). Since \(5\times 20=100\), multiply the top and bottom by \(20\). $$\frac{8}{5}=\frac{8\times 20}{5\times 20}=\frac{160}{100}.$$ A fraction over \(100\) reads straight off as a percent, so \(\tfrac{160}{100}=160\%\). You can check this by multiplying instead, \(\tfrac{8}{5}\times 100=\tfrac{800}{5}=160\). Because \(\tfrac{8}{5}\) is bigger than one whole, the percent climbed past \(100\), exactly as expected. \(\boxed{160}\)

blocks.x: A recipe for trail mix calls for \(\tfrac{9}{8}\) cups of oats, which is a little more than one full scoop. To put that on a label, the baker wants to write \(\tfrac{9}{8}\) as a percent of a single scoop. Since a percent is just the fraction multiplied by \(100\), and \(\tfrac{9}{8}\) is bigger than \(1\), the percent will land above \(100\), and because eighths split a whole into fine pieces, it will not be a round number. Write \(\tfrac{9}{8}\) as a percent. Give just the number.
hints: To turn a fraction into a percent, multiply it by \(100\). So you want \(\tfrac{9}{8}\times 100\), which is the same as \(\tfrac{900}{8}\).
Now simplify \(\tfrac{900}{8}\) by dividing top and bottom by \(4\) to get \(\tfrac{225}{2}\), then divide \(225\) by \(2\). It does not come out even, so expect a decimal ending in a half.
blocks.sol: A fraction becomes a percent when you multiply it by \(100\), so start with \(\tfrac{9}{8}\times 100\). Writing \(100\) over the eight gives $$\frac{9}{8}\times 100=\frac{9\times 100}{8}=\frac{900}{8}.$$ Divide the top and bottom by \(4\) to get \(\tfrac{225}{2}\), and dividing \(225\) by \(2\) gives \(112.5\). The result is more than \(100\) because \(\tfrac{9}{8}\) is more than one whole, and it carries a \(.5\) because halving an odd number leaves a half. So as a percent it is \(\boxed{112.5}\), meaning \(112.5\%\) of a single scoop.

blocks.x: Here is the big takeaway. A percent, a fraction, and a decimal are just three costumes for the very same number, so I can move between them whenever I like, sliding the point two places to change a decimal and a percent, or going over \(100\) to change a percent and a fraction. The real payoff is comparing. When two values show up in different costumes I cannot trust my eyes about which is bigger, so I dress them both in the same form first, usually all decimals or all over \(100\), and then the larger one is obvious. That is the whole power of knowing all three, one number, three looks, and I pick whichever look makes the question easy.

Two rooms voted on the snack for the end-of-year party, and both picked pizza by a landslide. In Room A, \(65\%\) of students chose pizza. In Room B, \(\tfrac{3}{5}\) of students chose pizza. These two shares are wearing different costumes, a percent and a fraction, so you cannot eyeball which is bigger yet. Rewrite both as decimals to put them in the same form, then decide. Option 1 is Room A at \(65\%\), option 2 is Room B at \(\tfrac{3}{5}\). Type the option number with the LARGER pizza share.
hints: You cannot compare a percent and a fraction while they look different. Turn both into decimals first. For the percent, slide the point two places left. For the fraction, divide the top by the bottom.
Room A is \(65\% = 0.65\). Room B is \(\tfrac{3}{5} = 0.60\). Now the costumes match, so just compare \(0.65\) with \(0.60\) and name the option that is bigger.
blocks.sol: Put both shares in the same form, decimals. Room A is \(65\%\). To turn a percent into a decimal, divide by \(100\), which slides the point two places left, so \(65\% = 0.65\). Room B is \(\tfrac{3}{5}\). Dividing \(3\) by \(5\) gives \(0.60\), and you can check it by rewriting \(\tfrac{3}{5} = \tfrac{6}{10} = 0.60\). Now both are decimals, so compare. Since \(0.65 > 0.60\), Room A has the larger pizza share, which is option 1. \(\boxed{1}\)

blocks.x: A receipt at a corner bakery lists the sales tax as \(12.5\%\), which is just a friendly way of writing twelve and a half percent. A percent is still a percent even when it carries a half, so it means \(12.5\) parts out of every hundred. Write \(12.5\%\) as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: Percent means per hundred, so start by writing \(12.5\%\) as \(\tfrac{12.5}{100}\). A fraction in lowest terms cannot keep a decimal upstairs, so you need to clear that half.
Multiply the top and bottom by \(10\) to get rid of the decimal point. That turns \(\tfrac{12.5}{100}\) into \(\tfrac{125}{1000}\), and now both numbers are whole and ready to simplify.
blocks.sol: Percent means per hundred, so \(12.5\%\) becomes \(\tfrac{12.5}{100}\). A tidy fraction has whole numbers top and bottom, so multiply both by \(10\) to slide the decimal away. That gives $$\frac{12.5}{100} = \frac{12.5 \times 10}{100 \times 10} = \frac{125}{1000}.$$ Now reduce. Both \(125\) and \(1{,}000\) share the factor \(125\), and dividing each by it gives \(\tfrac{1}{8}\). So twelve and a half percent is exactly one eighth, which makes sense since \(\tfrac{1}{8}\) of a hundred is \(12.5\). \(\boxed{1/8}\)

blocks.x: A purity label on a thin sheet of metal reads \(0.25\%\) impurity, a quarter of one percent, far below a single whole percent. That one tiny value wears all three costumes. As a fraction it begins as \(\tfrac{0.25}{100}\), which is the same as \(\tfrac{25}{10000}\), and that reduces step by step down to its simplest form. As a decimal the same value slides two places to the left and becomes \(0.0025\). Three forms, one number. Write \(0.25\%\) as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: A percent means per hundred, so \(0.25\%\) is \(\tfrac{0.25}{100}\). A fraction with a decimal on top is not yet finished, so clear that decimal first by multiplying the top and the bottom by the same number.
Multiply top and bottom by \(100\) to get \(\tfrac{25}{10000}\). Now divide top and bottom by \(25\) and see how clean the bottom becomes.
blocks.sol: Per hundred is the whole idea, so \(0.25\%\) means \(\tfrac{0.25}{100}\). A decimal in the numerator is not a finished fraction, so multiply the top and the bottom by \(100\) to clear it. $$\frac{0.25}{100}=\frac{0.25\times 100}{100\times 100}=\frac{25}{10000}$$ Now reduce. Both the top and the bottom are divisible by \(25\). $$\frac{25}{10000}=\frac{25\div 25}{10000\div 25}=\frac{1}{400}$$ As a quick check, sliding the point of \(0.25\%\) two places to the left gives the decimal \(0.0025\), and \(\tfrac{1}{400}=0.0025\), so the two forms agree. $$\boxed{1/400}$$

review.x: A survey found that \(55\%\) of students bike to school on a dry spring morning. Percent means "per hundred," so this is \(55\) out of \(100\). Write \(55\%\) as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: A percent is a fraction with a denominator of \(100\), so start by writing \(55\%\) as \(\tfrac{55}{100}\).
Both \(55\) and \(100\) share a factor of \(5\). Divide the top and the bottom by \(5\) to reach lowest terms.
review.sol: Percent means "per hundred," so \(55\%\) is \(55\) out of \(100\). $$55\% = \frac{55}{100}$$ Now reduce. The numerator and denominator both share a factor of \(5\), so divide each by \(5\). $$\frac{55}{100} = \frac{55 \div 5}{100 \div 5} = \frac{11}{20}$$ Since \(11\) is prime and does not divide \(20\), the fraction is in lowest terms. \(\boxed{11/20}\)

review.x: A pollster reports that \(24\%\) of the people she surveyed had never tasted dark chocolate. Remember that \(24\%\) just means \(24\) out of every \(100\). Write \(24\%\) as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: A percent is a count out of one hundred, so start by writing \(24\%\) as \(\tfrac{24}{100}\).
Both \(24\) and \(100\) are divisible by \(4\). Divide the top and the bottom by \(4\) and check that nothing else divides both.
review.sol: The word percent means per hundred, so \(24\%\) is \(24\) out of \(100\). $$24\% = \frac{24}{100}$$ Now simplify. The greatest factor shared by \(24\) and \(100\) is \(4\), so divide both the numerator and the denominator by \(4\). $$\frac{24}{100} = \frac{24 \div 4}{100 \div 4} = \frac{6}{25}$$ Since \(6\) and \(25\) share no common factor larger than \(1\), this is fully reduced. \(\boxed{6/25}\)

review.x: A loyalty card stamps your total at \(320\%\) of last month, a number bigger than a whole hundred. Write \(320\%\) as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: The word percent means per hundred, so \(320\%\) is just \(320\) over \(100\). Write that fraction first, then simplify.
Both \(320\) and \(100\) share the factor \(20\). Divide top and bottom by \(20\) to land in lowest terms.
review.sol: Percent means per hundred, so \(320\%=\tfrac{320}{100}\). The greatest common factor of \(320\) and \(100\) is \(20\), so divide both parts by it. That gives \(\tfrac{320\div 20}{100\div 20}=\tfrac{16}{5}\). Since \(16\) and \(5\) share no factor bigger than \(1\), this is lowest terms, and it is more than one whole because \(16>5\). \(\boxed{16/5}\)

review.x: A deep freezer logs how far its temperature drifts from the set point, and one reading came in at \(-8\%\). Write \(-8\%\) as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: A percent is a count out of one hundred, so \(-8\%\) means \(-8\) over \(100\). The negative sign rides along with the whole fraction.
Now reduce \(\tfrac{-8}{100}\). Both \(8\) and \(100\) share a factor of \(4\), so divide the top and the bottom by \(4\).
review.sol: Percent means per hundred, so the number sits over \(100\) and keeps its sign. That gives \(-8\% = \tfrac{-8}{100}\). The top and bottom share a common factor of \(4\), so divide both by \(4\). The numerator becomes \(-8 \div 4 = -2\) and the denominator becomes \(100 \div 4 = 25\). Since \(2\) and \(25\) have no common factor left, the fraction is in lowest terms. So \(-8\%\) equals \(\boxed{-2/25}\).

review.x: A weather app reports that the chance of rain tomorrow is \(73\%\). To use that chance in a calculation, you want it written as a decimal. Write \(73\%\) as a decimal.
hints: A percent means parts per hundred, so \(73\%\) is the same as \(\tfrac{73}{100}\).
Dividing by \(100\) slides the decimal point two places to the left, so \(73.\) becomes \(0.73\).
review.sol: A percent is just a number of hundredths, so \(73\%\) means \(\tfrac{73}{100}\). Dividing by \(100\) slides the decimal point two places to the left, turning \(73.\) into \(0.73\). So the decimal form is \(\boxed{0.73}\).

review.x: A percent can climb past one whole. Write \(108\%\) as a decimal.
hints: A percent means hundredths, so \(108\%\) is \(108\) hundredths. To turn a percent into a decimal, divide by \(100\), which slides the point two places left.
Start with \(108.\) and walk the decimal point two steps to the left, past the \(8\) and past the \(0\).
review.sol: A percent is a number out of \(100\), so to write it as a decimal you divide by \(100\), which slides the point two places to the left. Begin with \(108\), really \(108.0\), and move the point two places left to land between the \(1\) and the first \(0\). That gives \(108\% = \tfrac{108}{100} = 1.08\). Notice the result is more than \(1\), which makes sense because \(108\%\) is more than a whole. So the decimal is \(\boxed{1.08}\).

review.x: A lab label reads \(0.6\%\) saline, a very small slice of the whole. Write \(0.6\%\) as a decimal.
hints: A percent means per hundred, so \(0.6\%\) is the same as \(\tfrac{0.6}{100}\). Dividing by 100 slides the decimal point two places to the left.
Start at \(0.6\) and move the point two places left. You will need to write in placeholder zeros to fill the empty spots.
review.sol: A percent is just a number over 100, so \(0.6\%\) means \(\tfrac{0.6}{100}\). Dividing by 100 slides the decimal point two places to the left. Starting from \(0.6\), one place left gives \(0.06\), and a second place gives \(0.006\), with placeholder zeros filling the gaps. So \(0.6\%\) as a decimal is \(\boxed{0.006}\).

review.x: A scale at a science fair shows a probe filled to \(0.47\) of its tank, written as a decimal. The judges want that same amount posted on the board as a percent. Write \(0.47\) as a percent, just the number.
hints: A decimal becomes a percent when you multiply by \(100\). Multiplying by \(100\) slides the decimal point two places to the right.
Start at \(0.47\) and move the point past the \(4\) and the \(7\). Where does it land, and what whole number is left?
review.sol: To turn a decimal into a percent, multiply by \(100\), which slides the point two places to the right. So \(0.47 \times 100 = 47\), giving \(47\%\). You can also picture it as \(0.47 = \tfrac{47}{100}\), and a denominator of \(100\) reads straight off as \(47\) per hundred. The number to post is \(\boxed{47}\).

review.x: A jeweler's micro-scale shows that a single grain of gold dust registers \(0.025\) of a carat. The display has a mode that reports the same reading as a percent of one carat instead. Write \(0.025\) as a percent. Give just the number.
hints: Turning a decimal into a percent means multiplying by \(100\), which slides the decimal point two places to the right.
Start at \(0.025\) and move the point two places right. The first jump gives \(0.25\), the second gives \(2.5\), so the leftover digit keeps the percent a decimal.
review.sol: To go from a decimal to a percent, multiply by \(100\), which slides the decimal point two places to the right. $$0.025 \times 100 = 2.5$$ Sliding the point from \(0.025\) two places to the right lands between the \(2\) and the \(5\), so the value is \(2.5\%\). The number alone is \(\boxed{2.5}\).

review.x: A trail map shades \(\tfrac{7}{20}\) of its loops as beginner friendly. To compare that share against the trail signs, which all use percents, rewrite \(\tfrac{7}{20}\) so its denominator is \(100\). Write \(\tfrac{7}{20}\) as a percent, just the number.
hints: A percent is a count out of \(100\), so turn the bottom number into \(100\). What do you multiply \(20\) by to land on \(100\)?
Since \(20 \times 5 = 100\), multiply the top by \(5\) too. Then the numerator over \(100\) is your percent number.
review.sol: A percent is just a fraction with a denominator of \(100\), so the plan is to rewrite \(\tfrac{7}{20}\) with \(100\) on the bottom. Multiplying \(20\) by \(5\) gives \(100\), so multiply the top by \(5\) as well to keep the same value. $$\frac{7}{20} = \frac{7 \times 5}{20 \times 5} = \frac{35}{100}$$ A numerator of \(35\) out of \(100\) means \(35\%\), so the percent number is \(\boxed{35}\).

review.x: A juice recipe says \(\tfrac{5}{8}\) of the bottle is real fruit. To put that on the label, rewrite \(\tfrac{5}{8}\) as a percent. Write just the number.
hints: A fraction becomes a percent when you multiply by \(100\). So you want \(\tfrac{5}{8}\times 100\).
Compute \(\tfrac{5\times 100}{8}=\tfrac{500}{8}\), then divide. The result is not a whole number, so let it land on a decimal.
review.sol: To turn a fraction into a percent, multiply it by \(100\). $$\frac{5}{8}\times 100=\frac{500}{8}=62.5$$ So \(\tfrac{5}{8}\) of the bottle is \(62.5\%\) fruit, and the number is \(\boxed{62.5}\).

review.x: A fraction can land above 1, and it still has a percent form. Take the fraction \(\tfrac{11}{5}\). Write \(\tfrac{11}{5}\) as a percent, just the number.
hints: To turn any fraction into a percent, multiply it by \(100\). The result is how many parts per hundred the fraction is worth.
Compute \(\tfrac{11}{5}\times 100\). One clean route is \(11\times 100=1{,}100\), then divide by \(5\).
review.sol: A percent is just a count of parts per hundred, so to write a fraction as a percent you multiply it by \(100\). That gives \(\tfrac{11}{5}\times 100=\tfrac{1{,}100}{5}\). Dividing, \(1{,}100\div 5=220\), so \(\tfrac{11}{5}=220\%\). That makes sense, since \(\tfrac{11}{5}\) is more than \(2\), and \(220\%\) is more than \(200\%\). The number is \(\boxed{220}\).

review.x: A sign at a swim meet reads "the pool is \(87.5\%\) full." Write \(87.5\%\) as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: Percent means per hundred, so \(87.5\%\) is \(\tfrac{87.5}{100}\). Clear the decimal by multiplying the top and bottom by \(10\) to get \(\tfrac{875}{1000}\).
Both \(875\) and \(1{,}000\) share the factor \(125\). Divide each by \(125\) and see what is left.
review.sol: Percent means per hundred, so \(87.5\%=\tfrac{87.5}{100}\). Multiply the top and bottom by \(10\) to clear the decimal, which gives \(\tfrac{875}{1000}\). Now reduce. Both numbers are divisible by \(125\), and \(875\div125=7\) while \(1{,}000\div125=8\), so the fraction becomes \(\tfrac{7}{8}\). Since \(7\) is prime and does not divide \(8\), this is lowest terms. \(\boxed{7/8}\)
review.label: r13

review.x: Two values are dressed in different forms. Option 1 is the fraction \(\tfrac{9}{20}\), and option 2 is \(44\%\). Which value is larger? Type \(1\) or \(2\).
hints: To compare fairly, rewrite both as the same form. Turn the fraction into a percent so it sits next to \(44\%\).
Scale \(\tfrac{9}{20}\) up to a denominator of \(100\) by multiplying top and bottom by \(5\). What percent does that give you?
review.sol: Put both into percent form so they can be compared head to head. For the fraction, scale \(\tfrac{9}{20}\) to a denominator of \(100\) by multiplying top and bottom by \(5\), which gives \(\tfrac{45}{100}=45\%\). Now line them up, \(45\%\) against \(44\%\). Since \(45\%>44\%\), option 1 is the larger value. \(\boxed{1}\)

blocks.x: In 9.1 you learned to read a percent three ways, as a percent, a fraction, or a decimal, all standing for the same value. That was the grammar. This lesson is the first real sentence you build with it. Almost every percent question in the wild is one of three close cousins, and all three grow from a single relationship. When someone asks for a percent of a number, that little word "of" is quietly telling you to multiply. A jacket marked 25% off, a tip that is 18% of the bill, a poll where 60% of voters agree, each one is a percent multiplied by a whole amount to give a part. So the backbone of the whole lesson is this, the part equals the percent times the whole. Once you see that one relationship, the three questions are just three ways of leaving one piece blank. You might know the percent and the whole and want the part, or know the part and the whole and want the percent, or know the part and the percent and want the whole. Same relationship every time, read from a different corner.

Start with the friendliest version, where you know the percent and the whole and just want the part. A wildlife reserve counts \(200\) nesting birds, and a survey finds that \(25\%\) of them are herons. To find how many herons that is, turn the word "of" into multiplication, since \(25\%\) of \(200\) means \(25\%\) times \(200\). Convert the percent to the fraction \(\tfrac{1}{4}\) or the decimal \(0.25\), then multiply. How many herons are there?
hints: The word "of" means multiply. Rewrite \(25\%\) as \(\tfrac{1}{4}\) or \(0.25\), then multiply it by \(200\).
One quarter of \(200\) is \(200 \div 4 = 50\). Or \(0.25 \times 200 = 50\).
blocks.sol: A percent of a number is a multiplication, with "of" as the times sign. First convert the percent, so \(25\% = \tfrac{1}{4} = 0.25\). Then multiply by the whole, $$25\% \text{ of } 200 = \tfrac{1}{4} \times 200 = 50.$$ The same answer comes from \(0.25 \times 200 = 50\). So there are \(\boxed{50}\) herons.

blocks.x: Here is the move at the heart of it. To find a percent of a number, turn the percent into a fraction or a decimal and then multiply, because the word "of" sitting between a percent and a number always means multiply. This is just part = percent times whole, with the percent and the whole known and the part waiting to be found.

A bakery sold \(35\) loaves yesterday, and \(60\%\) of them were sourdough. To find the number of sourdough loaves, multiply the whole by the percent. Convert \(60\%\) to \(\tfrac{3}{5}\) or \(0.6\) first, then multiply by \(35\). How many sourdough loaves were sold?
hints: Rewrite \(60\%\) as \(0.6\) (or \(\tfrac{3}{5}\)), then multiply by \(35\).
blocks.sol: Convert and multiply. Since \(60\% = \tfrac{3}{5} = 0.6\), $$60\% \text{ of } 35 = \tfrac{3}{5} \times 35 = 3 \times 7 = 21.$$ So \(\boxed{21}\) sourdough loaves were sold.

blocks.x: Percents are not capped at \(100\). A small startup had \(15\) employees last year and grew to \(300\%\) of that size this year. To find this year's count, multiply \(15\) by \(300\%\). Convert \(300\%\) to the whole number \(3\) first, since \(300\% = \tfrac{300}{100} = 3\), then multiply. How many employees are there this year?
hints: A percent over \(100\) becomes a number bigger than \(1\). Here \(300\% = 3\).
Multiply \(3 \times 15 = 45\).
blocks.sol: Convert the percent, \(300\% = \tfrac{300}{100} = 3\). Then $$300\% \text{ of } 15 = 3 \times 15 = 45.$$ Notice the answer is larger than the original \(15\), which makes sense because a percent above \(100\) gives more than the whole. So there are \(\boxed{45}\) employees.

blocks.x: Watch what the size of the percent does. Taking \(100\%\) of a number leaves it unchanged, taking less than \(100\%\) gives less than the number, and taking more than \(100\%\) gives more, since you are multiplying by exactly \(1\), by less than \(1\), or by more than \(1\).

blocks.cap: Finding \(25\%\) of \(200\). Split the whole bar of \(200\) into four equal quarters of \(50\). One quarter is \(25\%\), so \(25\%\) of \(200\) is one of those quarters, \(\tfrac{1}{4} \times 200 = 50\). Taking a percent of a number is multiplying the whole by that fraction.

blocks.x: A percent can carry a fraction in it. A theater holds \(64\) seats, and \(12\tfrac{1}{2}\%\) of them are reserved for staff. The cleanest path is to convert \(12\tfrac{1}{2}\%\) to a fraction, and since \(12\tfrac{1}{2}\% = \tfrac{1}{8}\), you just take one eighth of \(64\). How many seats are reserved?
hints: One eighth of \(64\) is \(64 \div 8 = 8\).
blocks.sol: Convert the fractional percent to a clean fraction, \(12\tfrac{1}{2}\% = \tfrac{12.5}{100} = \tfrac{1}{8}\). Then $$12\tfrac{1}{2}\% \text{ of } 64 = \tfrac{1}{8} \times 64 = 8.$$ So \(\boxed{8}\) seats are reserved.

blocks.x: Tiny percents work the same way. A water sample weighs \(80\) grams, and \(2.5\%\) of it is dissolved minerals. Convert \(2.5\%\) to the decimal \(0.025\), then multiply by \(80\). How many grams of dissolved minerals are there?
hints: Slide the point two places left, so \(2.5\% = 0.025\).
blocks.sol: Convert the percent to a decimal by sliding the point two places left, \(2.5\% = 0.025\). Then $$2.5\% \text{ of } 80 = 0.025 \times 80 = 2.$$ So there are \(\boxed{2}\) grams of dissolved minerals.

blocks.x: Now flip the question. This time you know the part and the whole, and you want the percent. A class read \(30\) books out of a goal of \(120\) books. To find what percent of the goal they reached, divide the part by the whole and write the result as a percent. What percent is \(30\) of \(120\)? Give just the number.
hints: Make the fraction part over whole, \(\tfrac{30}{120}\), and simplify.
blocks.sol: To find what percent one number is of another, divide the part by the whole, then write it as a percent. $$\tfrac{30}{120} = \tfrac{1}{4} = 0.25 = 25\%.$$ So \(30\) is \(\boxed{25}\) percent of \(120\).

blocks.x: Reading the relationship from a different corner, to find what percent one number is of another, divide the part by the whole and write the answer as a percent. That is just percent = part divided by whole, since a percent is the part measured against the whole and then scaled to a hundred.

A phone battery holds a charge of \(80\) units when full, and right now it holds \(60\) units. To report the battery level as a percent, divide the current charge by the full charge and convert. What percent of \(80\) is \(60\)? Give just the number.
hints: The part is \(60\) and the whole is \(80\), so form \(\tfrac{60}{80}\) and simplify.
blocks.sol: Divide the part by the whole, then convert to a percent. $$\tfrac{60}{80} = \tfrac{3}{4} = 0.75 = 75\%.$$ So \(60\) is \(\boxed{75}\) percent of \(80\).

blocks.x: The part can be bigger than the whole, which just gives a percent over \(100\). A second-hand shop bought a desk for \(17\) dollars and resold it for \(51\) dollars. To see the resale price as a percent of the cost, divide \(51\) by \(17\). What percent is \(51\) of \(17\)? Give just the number.
hints: Form \(\tfrac{51}{17}\). Since \(51\) is bigger than \(17\), expect a percent above \(100\).
blocks.sol: Divide the part by the whole, \(\tfrac{51}{17} = 3\). As a percent that is \(3 = 300\%\). The resale price is three times the cost, so \(51\) is \(\boxed{300}\) percent of \(17\).

The part sits at the top, the percent at the lower left, and the whole at the lower right. The relationships are part equals percent times whole, percent equals part divided by whole, and whole equals part divided by percent. Cover the one you want and the other two tell you how to find it.">

  <text x="68" y="148" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="10.5" fill="var(--slate)">/ percent</text>

blocks.cap: The three percent questions are one relationship, \(\text{part} = \text{percent} \times \text{whole}\), read from three corners. Cover the part and you multiply percent by whole. Cover the percent and you divide the part by the whole. Cover the whole and you divide the part by the percent.

blocks.x: Here is the third question, where you know the part and the percent but not the whole. A scholarship covers \(80\) dollars of a fee, and that \(80\) dollars is exactly \(20\%\) of the full fee. To recover the whole fee, undo the multiplication by dividing the part by the percent. Since \(80\) is \(20\%\) of the fee, the fee is \(80 \div 0.2\). \(80\) is \(20\%\) of what number?
hints: The relationship is part equals percent times whole, so whole equals part divided by percent. Use \(20\% = 0.2\).
blocks.sol: Since the part is the percent times the whole, the whole is the part divided by the percent. With \(20\% = 0.2\), $$\text{whole} = \frac{80}{0.2} = 400.$$ Check it, \(20\%\) of \(400\) is \(0.2 \times 400 = 80\), which matches. So \(80\) is \(20\%\) of \(\boxed{400}\).

blocks.x: The last corner. When you know the part and the percent but not the whole, divide the part by the percent, written as a fraction or decimal, to recover the whole. This is whole = part divided by percent, the reverse of multiplying, so always check by taking that percent of your answer to see if it lands back on the part.

A hiker has walked \(27\) kilometers, which is \(75\%\) of the whole trail. To find the trail's full length, divide the part by the percent. Use \(75\% = \tfrac{3}{4}\) or \(0.75\). \(27\) is \(75\%\) of what number?
hints: Whole equals part divided by percent, so compute \(27 \div 0.75\).
blocks.sol: Divide the part by the percent. With \(75\% = \tfrac{3}{4}\), $$\text{whole} = 27 \div \tfrac{3}{4} = 27 \times \tfrac{4}{3} = 36.$$ Check, \(\tfrac{3}{4} \times 36 = 27\), which matches. The trail is \(\boxed{36}\) kilometers long.

blocks.x: When the percent is over \(100\), the whole comes out smaller than the part. A startup's revenue this month is \(18\) thousand dollars, which is \(150\%\) of last month's revenue. To find last month's revenue, divide the part by the percent, using \(150\% = 1.5\). \(18\) is \(150\%\) of what number?
hints: Whole equals part divided by percent, so compute \(18 \div 1.5\).
\(18 \div 1.5 = 12\). Since the percent is over \(100\), the whole is smaller than the part.
blocks.sol: Divide the part by the percent, with \(150\% = 1.5\). $$\text{whole} = \frac{18}{1.5} = 12.$$ Because \(150\%\) is more than \(100\%\), the answer \(12\) is smaller than the part \(18\), which fits. Check, \(1.5 \times 12 = 18\). So last month's revenue was \(\boxed{12}\) thousand dollars.

blocks.x: Percents have been doing tax work for a very long time. In ancient Rome the emperor Augustus funded the army with a tax called the centesima rerum venalium, a "hundredth of things sold", which was a \(1\%\) levy on goods at public auction. Roman records are full of such hundredth-based rates, charged on sales, on freeing enslaved people, and on inheritances. Counting in hundredths made these shares easy to compare and collect, the very same reason a percent is handy today, and it is part of why the language of percent grew up so closely tied to money.

Bring the three questions together in one chain. A museum survey finds that \(30\%\) of \(80\) visitors came by bus, and you want to know what percent of a different group, \(120\) school visitors, that same bus count represents. First find the part, the number of bus visitors, by taking \(30\%\) of \(80\). Then ask what percent that part is of \(120\). What percent of \(120\) is \(30\%\) of \(80\)? Give just the number.
hints: Step one, find \(30\%\) of \(80\). That is \(0.3 \times 80 = 24\).
Step two, what percent is \(24\) of \(120\)? Form \(\tfrac{24}{120} = 0.2 = 20\%\).
blocks.sol: Do it in two steps. First the part, \(30\%\) of \(80 = 0.3 \times 80 = 24\). Then ask what percent \(24\) is of \(120\), $$\tfrac{24}{120} = \tfrac{1}{5} = 0.2 = 20\%.$$ So \(30\%\) of \(80\) is \(\boxed{20}\) percent of \(120\).

review.x: A farmers market had \(90\) stalls, and \(40\%\) of them sold vegetables. How many stalls sold vegetables?
hints: "Of" means multiply. Rewrite \(40\% = 0.4\), then multiply by \(90\).
review.sol: \(40\% = 0.4\), so \(40\% \text{ of } 90 = 0.4 \times 90 = 36\). So \(\boxed{36}\) stalls sold vegetables.

review.x: A song had \(12\) thousand listeners last week and climbed to \(250\%\) of that this week. How many thousand listeners is that this week?
hints: A percent over \(100\) is more than \(1\). Here \(250\% = 2.5\).
review.sol: \(250\% = \tfrac{250}{100} = 2.5\), so \(250\% \text{ of } 12 = 2.5 \times 12 = 30\). So there are \(\boxed{30}\) thousand listeners.

review.x: A puzzle has \(16\) pieces, and \(37\tfrac{1}{2}\%\) of them are edge pieces. How many edge pieces are there?
hints: Convert the fractional percent, \(37\tfrac{1}{2}\% = \tfrac{37.5}{100} = \tfrac{3}{8}\).
review.sol: \(37\tfrac{1}{2}\% = \tfrac{37.5}{100} = \tfrac{3}{8}\), so \(\tfrac{3}{8} \times 16 = 6\). So there are \(\boxed{6}\) edge pieces.

review.x: A reservoir holds \(150\) million liters, and \(8\%\) of it is held in reserve. How many million liters are in reserve?
hints: Rewrite \(8\% = 0.08\), then multiply by \(150\).
review.sol: \(8\% = 0.08\), so \(8\% \text{ of } 150 = 0.08 \times 150 = 12\). So \(\boxed{12}\) million liters are in reserve.

review.x: A team won \(18\) of its \(72\) games. What percent of its games did the team win? Give just the number.
hints: Form part over whole, \(\tfrac{18}{72}\), and simplify.
review.sol: \(\tfrac{18}{72} = \tfrac{1}{4} = 0.25 = 25\%\). So the team won \(\boxed{25}\) percent of its games.

review.x: A jar holds \(40\) marbles, and \(30\) of them are blue. What percent of \(40\) is \(30\)? Give just the number.
hints: Form \(\tfrac{30}{40}\) and simplify.

review.x: A sequel sold \(84\) thousand copies, while the original sold \(56\) thousand. What percent is \(84\) of \(56\)? Give just the number.
hints: Form \(\tfrac{84}{56}\). Since \(84\) is bigger than \(56\), expect over \(100\).

review.x: A deposit of \(21\) dollars is \(30\%\) of the total price of a bike. \(21\) is \(30\%\) of what number?
hints: Whole equals part divided by percent, so compute \(21 \div 0.3\).
review.sol: Whole equals part divided by percent, with \(30\% = 0.3\), so \(\tfrac{21}{0.3} = 70\). Check, \(0.3 \times 70 = 21\). So the price is \(\boxed{70}\) dollars.

review.x: A runner has finished \(45\) laps, which is \(90\%\) of the race. \(45\) is \(90\%\) of what number?
hints: Compute \(45 \div 0.9\).
review.sol: Whole equals part divided by percent, with \(90\% = 0.9\), so \(\tfrac{45}{0.9} = 50\). Check, \(0.9 \times 50 = 45\). So the race is \(\boxed{50}\) laps.

review.x: This year a town recorded \(120\) building permits, which is \(150\%\) of last year. \(120\) is \(150\%\) of what number?
hints: Compute \(120 \div 1.5\). Since the percent is over \(100\), the whole is smaller than \(120\).
review.sol: Whole equals part divided by percent, with \(150\% = 1.5\), so \(\tfrac{120}{1.5} = 80\). Check, \(1.5 \times 80 = 120\). So last year had \(\boxed{80}\) permits.

review.x: A tasting portion is \(5\) grams, which the recipe says is \(12\tfrac{1}{2}\%\) of a full serving. \(5\) is \(12\tfrac{1}{2}\%\) of what number?
hints: Convert \(12\tfrac{1}{2}\% = \tfrac{1}{8}\), then whole equals part divided by percent.
review.sol: Convert the percent, \(12\tfrac{1}{2}\% = \tfrac{1}{8}\). Whole equals part divided by percent, so \(5 \div \tfrac{1}{8} = 5 \times 8 = 40\). Check, \(\tfrac{1}{8} \times 40 = 5\). So a full serving is \(\boxed{40}\) grams.

review.x: Chain two steps together. First take \(60\%\) of \(500\), then take \(20\%\) of that result. What is \(20\%\) of \(60\%\) of \(500\)?

blocks.x: In 9.2 you took a percent of a number and got back a part, a still snapshot of one quantity sitting quietly at one size. Now the quantity itself starts to move. A rooftop garden lengthens its little walking trail over the summer, a flock of marsh birds thins out in a heat wave, a kayak rental drops its price for a slow week, a podcast watches its weekly plays climb. Each of these is a number going up or down by some percent, and one idea runs through every single case. The change is always measured against the amount you started with, the original, never the amount you finish at. To raise a quantity by a percent you take that percent of the original and add it on, and to lower it you take that percent of the original and subtract it away. So a percent change is really just 9.2 again, since taking a percent of a number is a multiply and the part you add or remove is \(\text{percent} \times \text{whole}\), with the original playing the role of the whole. Here is the shortcut that will carry the whole lesson. Raising something by \(30\%\) means you keep the entire original, all \(100\%\) of it, and then stack another \(30\%\) on top, so the new amount is \(130\%\) of the original. That whole two-step move collapses into a single multiply by \(1.30\), one clean number called the multiplier. Three journeys are coming. First you will travel forward, from an old amount to a new one. Then you will run the change backward, recovering the original you started from when you only know where you landed. And finally there is the strange ending, where a rise of some percent followed by an equal fall does not bring you home, because each change quietly measures itself against a different original. No numbers solved yet, just the lay of the land.

A rooftop community garden started the season with \(240\) meters of walking trail. Over the summer the volunteers extended it by \(35\%\), which means they added \(35\%\) of the original \(240\) meters and then tacked that onto the \(240\) they already had. Take it one step at a time. First find \(35\%\) of \(240\) the way you did in 9.2, then add that piece back to the starting length. After this increase, how many meters of trail are there now?
hints: The extra trail is \(35\%\) of the ORIGINAL \(240\) meters, not of some new total. Use part \(=\) percent \(\times\) whole, so the added length is \(0.35 \times 240\).
Once you know the added piece is \(84\) meters, the new trail is just the old length plus that piece, \(240 + 84\). Adding the extra back on is what makes this an increase.
blocks.sol: Walk it the slow way first. The increase is \(35\%\) of the original \(240\) meters, so using part \(=\) percent \(\times\) whole gives $$0.35 \times 240 = 84 \text{ meters added.}$$ Now add that piece back onto where you started, \(240 + 84 = 324\). There is a faster route that lands in the same place. Keeping all of the original is \(100\%\) of it, and adding \(35\%\) more makes \(135\%\) in all, so the multiplier is \(1 + 0.35 = 1.35\). That gives \(240 \times 1.35 = 324\) in one step. Either way the trail is now \(\boxed{324}\) meters long.

blocks.x: When something grows by a percent, you do not just glue the percent on. You take that percent OF the amount you started with and add it back onto that same starting amount, so the new total is the original plus a slice of itself. The slice is always measured from where you began, never from where you land. And since the original is already the full \(100\%\) of itself and we just piled more on top, a \(30\%\) rise should be the same as keeping all \(100\%\) and adding \(30\%\) more, which is \(130\%\) of the start, hinting that the two steps might fold into one quick multiply.

A small streaming channel sat at \(480\) subscribers. One post took off and the channel grew by \(35\%\). You could find \(35\%\) of \(480\) and add it on, and that works, but watch for a faster route. The channel already has all of itself, which is \(100\%\), and you are stacking another \(35\%\) on top, so the new count is \(135\%\) of \(480\). That is the same as \(1 + \tfrac{35}{100} = 1.35\) times \(480\), done in one step. After the \(35\%\) increase, how many subscribers does the channel have now?
hints: The original count is \(100\%\) of itself, and an increase piles more on top of that. A \(35\%\) increase means the new amount is \(100\% + 35\% = 135\%\) of the original.
Turn \(135\%\) into the multiplier \(1.35\), then compute \(480 \times 1.35\) in one step instead of adding the part separately.
blocks.sol: A \(35\%\) increase keeps the whole original and adds \(35\%\) more, so the new count is \(100\% + 35\% = 135\%\) of \(480\). Written as a multiplier that is \(1 + \tfrac{35}{100} = 1.35\). Multiply all at once. $$480 \times 1.35 = 648$$ Check it against the add-on path. \(35\%\) of \(480\) is \(0.35 \times 480 = 168\), and \(480 + 168 = 648\), the same result. The channel now has \(\boxed{648}\) subscribers.

blocks.x: Here is the shortcut I just stumbled onto. When something grows by \(30\%\), I am keeping all \(100\%\) of what I started with and stacking another \(30\%\) on top, which is really \(130\%\) of the original, so I can skip the two steps and just multiply once by \(1.30\). The \(1\) is the part that hangs onto the whole original amount, and the \(0.30\) is the extra slice the increase adds, which is why growing by \(p\) percent always means multiplying by \(1 + \tfrac{p}{100}\). This one multiplier turns a percent increase into a single move, and it is the tool I will reach for again and again in this lesson.

blocks.cap: Both strips start from the same original, the full \(100\%\) bar. Adding \(30\%\) stacks a new block on the right, so the total is \(100\% + 30\% = 130\%\), which is the multiplier \(\times 1.30\). Cutting \(20\%\) peels a slice off the right, leaving \(100\% - 20\% = 80\%\), the multiplier \(\times 0.80\). An increase always lands above \(1\) and a decrease always lands below \(1\), and either way you are scaling the original \(100\%\).

blocks.x: A morning survey of a coastal marsh counted \(350\) wading birds standing in the shallows. By the heat of midday, \(12\%\) of them had lifted off and flown elsewhere. A decrease keeps what is LEFT behind, and what stays is \(100\%\) minus \(12\%\), so \(88\%\) of the flock remains. That makes the midday count \(350\) times \(0.88\), and it should also match \(350\) minus \(12\%\) of \(350\). After this decrease, how many wading birds are still present at midday?
hints: A \(12\%\) decrease does not leave \(12\%\); it leaves the rest. Find the leftover percent by taking \(100\% - 12\% = 88\%\), and that fraction of the flock is what you want.

Turn \(88\%\) into the multiplier \(0.88\) and multiply the original count by it, \(350 \times 0.88\). If you prefer the long way, take \(12\%\) of \(350\) and subtract it from \(350\); both roads land on the same number.
blocks.sol: A drop of \(12\%\) takes away \(12\%\) and leaves the other \(88\%\), since \(100\% - 12\% = 88\%\). So the multiplier for a \(12\%\) decrease is \(1 - 0.12 = 0.88\), and the midday count is $$350 \times 0.88 = 308.$$ The slower route agrees. First \(12\%\) of \(350\) is \(0.12 \times 350 = 42\) birds that flew off, and the survivors are \(350 - 42 = 308\). Both paths give the same flock, so \(308\) wading birds are still present at midday. \(\boxed{308}\)

blocks.x: A harbor rents kayaks for \(90\) dollars a day. On a slow weekday it posts a \(40\%\) discount to pull people in. A discount works just like an increase running in reverse. Taking \(40\%\) off means \(40\%\) of the price is removed, so the part you actually pay is the \(60\%\) that survives, which is \(90\) times \(0.60\). Notice the parallel with the increase you saw earlier. There the multiplier climbed above \(1\), and here it drops below \(1\). What is the discounted price in dollars (a decrease)?
hints: A \(40\%\) discount removes \(40\%\) of the price, so what is left is \(100\% - 40\% = 60\%\) of it. That surviving \(60\%\) is your multiplier.
The multiplier for a \(40\%\) decrease is \(1 - 0.40 = 0.60\). Multiply the original price by it, \(90 \times 0.60\).
blocks.sol: A \(40\%\) decrease multiplies by \(1 - \tfrac{40}{100} = 1 - 0.40 = 0.60\), the share of the price that survives the discount. So the new price is $$90 \times 0.60 = 54.$$ You can check it the slow way. Forty percent of \(90\) is \(0.40 \times 90 = 36\), and subtracting gives \(90 - 36 = 54\), the same answer. The multiplier just folds the subtracting into one step. \(\boxed{54}\)

blocks.x: Now run it the other way. When you cut a percent off, what is left behind is the rest of the hundred, so removing \(12\%\) leaves \(88\%\) standing, a single multiply by \(0.88\). A \(p\) percent decrease multiplies by \(1 - \tfrac{p}{100}\), which is why a \(20\%\) discount is just \(80\%\) of the original price, one multiply by \(0.80\). Increase lifts the multiplier above \(1\) and decrease pulls it below \(1\), but it is the same one-multiply move in both directions.

A new podcast pulled in \(250\) plays on its first episode. The second episode did better and reached \(320\) plays. To measure the jump, start with the change, \(320 - 250 = 70\) extra plays. Now here is the key part. A percent change always asks what fraction the change is of where you began, so you compare those \(70\) plays to the starting \(250\), not to the new \(320\). What percent increase in plays was this? Give the number only.
hints: First find the change in plays, the new amount minus the old amount. Then remember a percent change is measured against the ORIGINAL, so the comparison you want is that change divided by \(250\), not divided by \(320\).
You have \(70\) more plays out of the starting \(250\). Compute \(\tfrac{70}{250}\) and turn that fraction into a percent by multiplying by \(100\).
blocks.sol: First find how much the plays grew, \(320 - 250 = 70\) more plays. A percent change is that change measured against the original amount, so divide by the \(250\) you started with, not the \(320\) you ended with. That gives the fraction \(\tfrac{70}{250}\). To read it as a percent, multiply by \(100\), so $$\frac{70}{250} \times 100 = 28.$$ You can check it with the multiplier idea. A \(28\%\) increase scales the original by \(1.28\), and \(250 \times 1.28 = 320\), which lands exactly on the second episode. The question names the direction, so the answer is the positive size of the increase, \(\boxed{28}\).

blocks.x: A small library branch buzzed on a sunny Saturday with \(640\) visitors. The next Saturday it rained all day and only \(480\) people came in. So the count fell from \(640\) down to \(480\), a drop of \(640 - 480 = 160\) visitors. To turn that drop into a percent, compare it to what you started with, the original \(640\), not the smaller rainy-day \(480\). Dividing by \(480\) would secretly be asking a different question. Because the number of visitors shrank, this is a decrease. What percent decrease was this? Give the number only.
hints: First find the drop, then ask what fraction of the ORIGINAL it is. The drop is \(640 - 480 = 160\), and the original is \(640\), so the percent change is \(\tfrac{160}{640}\) written as a percent.

Simplify \(\tfrac{160}{640}\). Both top and bottom share a factor of \(160\), so it becomes \(\tfrac{1}{4}\), and \(\tfrac{1}{4} = 25\%\). Resist the urge to divide by \(480\); the original count is the whole here.
blocks.sol:
The drop in visitors is \(640 - 480 = 160\). A percent change always measures that change against the original amount, so divide by \(640\), not by \(480\).

$$\frac{640 - 480}{640} = \frac{160}{640} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 = 25\%$$

You can check it with the multiplier. A drop of \(25\%\) means the rainy Saturday kept \(1 - 0.25 = 0.75\) of the sunny crowd, and \(0.75 \times 640 = 480\), which matches. If you had divided by \(480\) instead you would get about \(33\%\), but that answers a different question, how much you would need to grow to climb back to \(640\). For the decrease from the original, the answer is \(\boxed{25}\).

blocks.x: Here is what you just pinned down. To find the percent change from an old amount to a new one, first find how much it changed, the new minus the old, and then divide that by the original amount and write the answer as a percent. It is a percent increase when the number grew and a percent decrease when it shrank, and the size of the change is the same either way. The original is always the yardstick sitting in the denominator, since the question is how big the change was compared to where you started, so dividing by the new amount would quietly answer a completely different question.

blocks.cap: A podcast climbs from \(250\) plays to \(320\). The gold slice is the change, \(320 - 250 = 70\), and the percent change is that change measured against where we started, \(\tfrac{70}{250} = 0.28 = 28\%\). Dividing by the new amount, \(\tfrac{70}{320}\), is the trap, since the change is always compared to the original, never to where you landed.

blocks.x: A bamboo shoot stood \(14\) centimeters tall at dawn. Bamboo grows famously fast, and by the next dawn it had stretched to \(35\) centimeters. To find the percent change, take the change, \(35 - 14 = 21\) centimeters, and compare it to the original \(14\) centimeters. Watch the size of this jump. A true doubling, from \(14\) up to \(28\), would already be a \(100\%\) increase, and this shoot grew even more than double, so the answer will sail right past \(100\%\). What percent increase was this? Give the number only.
hints: The change is \(35 - 14 = 21\) centimeters. For a percent change you always divide by the ORIGINAL amount, the \(14\) centimeters it started at, not the \(35\) it ended at.
Compute \(\tfrac{21}{14}\) and turn it into a percent. Since \(21\) is more than \(14\), this fraction is bigger than \(1\), so the percent lands above \(100\%\).
blocks.sol: First find the change, \(35 - 14 = 21\) centimeters. A percent change is measured against the original, so divide by \(14\), not by \(35\). $$\frac{35 - 14}{14} = \frac{21}{14} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5$$ As a percent that is \(150\%\). You can sanity check it with the multiplier. Going from \(14\) to \(35\) means multiplying by \(\tfrac{35}{14} = 2.5\), and a multiplier of \(2.5\) is the same as \(1 + 1.5\), a \(150\%\) increase. That fits the warning in the problem. A plain doubling to \(28\) would have been a \(100\%\) increase, and this rose past double, so the percent climbs above \(100\%\). \(\boxed{150}\)

blocks.x: When something doubles, you have added one whole extra copy of what you started with, so that is a \(100\%\) increase, not \(200\%\). Tripling adds two more copies, a \(200\%\) increase. There is no top end at all, since any increase past \(100\%\) just means the new amount has climbed past double the old, and an amount can keep growing as far as you like. A decrease is the opposite. It has a floor, because once you have lost \(100\%\) there is nothing left to take, so a drop can never beat \(100\%\) while a rise can run as high as the growth itself.

Here is a slippery little distinction that trips up even careful adults. Suppose a savings account pays \(5\%\) one year and then \(7\%\) the next. That jump is a rise of 2 PERCENTAGE POINTS, which is just the plain gap \(7 - 5\) between the two rates. But measured as a percent INCREASE it is much bigger, because you divide the gap by the original rate, and \(2 \div 5 = 0.40\), a \(40\%\) increase. Percentage points compare two rates by subtracting, while a percent change divides that gap by the original. This is exactly why a headline about a rate can sound tiny or huge depending on which one the writer reaches for, so when you read about rates moving, it pays to ask which kind of change they mean.

The local-history shelf at a town library is overflowing this summer. After the librarians grew the collection by \(20\%\) over last year, the shelf now holds \(738\) books. A volunteer wants to know last year's count and reaches for the obvious move, taking \(20\%\) off the \(738\). Watch out, because that \(20\%\) was measured against the OLD number, not the new \(738\). Finding the original before the increase, how many books were on the shelf last year?
hints: The tempting wrong path is \(738 - 20\%\) of \(738 = 590.4\), which is not even a whole number of books. The \(20\%\) growth was a slice of last year's count, not of this year's. So the right question is what number, when grown by \(20\%\), lands on \(738\).
A \(20\%\) increase multiplies the original by \(1.20\), so \(1.20 \times \text{original} = 738\). To undo a multiplication, divide. Compute \(738 \div 1.20\).
blocks.sol: First see why subtracting fails. The wrong path gives \(738 - 0.20 \times 738 = 738 - 147.6 = 590.4\), and a shelf cannot hold \(590.4\) books. The reason is that the \(20\%\) was a slice of LAST year's count, not of this year's \(738\), so peeling \(20\%\) off the new total takes off too much. Set it up with the multiplier instead. A \(20\%\) increase means the new amount is \(1.20\) times the original, so $$1.20 \times \text{original} = 738.$$ Undo the multiplication by dividing both sides by \(1.20\), which gives \(\text{original} = 738 \div 1.20 = 615\). Check it by growing \(615\) by \(20\%\), since \(1.20 \times 615 = 615 + 123 = 738\), exactly the new count. Last year the shelf held \(\boxed{615}\) books.

blocks.x: Here is the thing I kept getting wrong until it clicked. To get back to the amount before a percent change, I divide the new amount by the multiplier, because the new amount is the original times that multiplier and dividing is what undoes a multiply. What I cannot do is just subtract the same percent off the new amount, since that percent was a slice of the smaller original, and subtracting it from the bigger new number peels off too much. So after a \(20\%\) increase the new amount is \(1.20\) times the original, which means I divide by \(1.20\) to climb back, not knock \(20\%\) off the new price.

A ferry line announced a \(15\%\) fare cut, and now a full season pass costs \(510\) dollars. The cut took the old price and left behind \(85\%\) of it. What was the original season pass price in dollars, before the decrease?
hints: A \(15\%\) decrease keeps \(100\% - 15\% = 85\%\) of the original, so it multiplies the old price by \(0.85\) to give \(510\). The \(510\) is the new amount, not the original.

Since \(\text{original} \times 0.85 = 510\), undo the multiplier by dividing. Compute \(510 \div 0.85\). Adding \(15\%\) of \(510\) back on is the wrong move, because that \(15\%\) would be a slice of the smaller current price, not of the original.
blocks.sol: A \(15\%\) decrease leaves \(100\% - 15\% = 85\%\) of the original price, so the multiplier is \(0.85\). That multiplier acted on the old price to produce the current one, which means \(\text{original} \times 0.85 = 510\). To recover the original, divide the new amount by the multiplier. $$\text{original} = \frac{510}{0.85} = 600$$ Check it forward, \(600 \times 0.85 = 510\), which matches. Notice why the tempting shortcut fails. Adding \(15\%\) of \(510\) back gives \(510 + 76.50 = 586.50\), an overshoot, because \(15\%\) of the smaller current price is smaller than \(15\%\) of the original. The original season pass price was \(\boxed{600}\) dollars.

blocks.x: A backyard bee colony has \(200\) workers. One warm, flower-filled week the colony grew by \(30\%\). Then a cold snap hit, and the next week the colony shrank by \(30\%\). A lot of people guess it must be right back at \(200\), since it went up \(30\%\) and then down \(30\%\). How many workers are in the colony at the end?
hints: Do the two weeks in order, one change at a time. A \(30\%\) increase multiplies by \(1.30\), so the boosted colony is \(200 \times 1.30\). Whatever that comes to is the new starting size for week two.

For week two, a \(30\%\) decrease multiplies by \(0.70\). The catch is that the \(30\%\) is taken off the bigger boosted number, not off the original \(200\), so it removes more bees than the increase added.
blocks.sol: Handle the weeks in order, each as a multiplier. A \(30\%\) increase multiplies by \(1.30\), and a \(30\%\) decrease multiplies by \(0.70\). After week one, \(200 \times 1.30 = 260\) workers. After week two, the cold snap takes \(30\%\) off that boosted \(260\), so \(260 \times 0.70 = 182\). Notice the two multipliers chain together as \(1.30 \times 0.70 = 0.91\), which is less than \(1\), so the colony lands below where it began. The \(30\%\) drop was taken from the larger number \(260\), so it stripped away more bees than the earlier \(30\%\) boost had added. The colony ends at \(\boxed{182}\) workers.

blocks.x: Here is the surprise that two changes in a row hand you. When one percent change follows another, you do not add the percents, you multiply the multipliers, because the second change is taken of a different, bigger or smaller amount than the first. That is why a \(p\) percent increase and then a \(p\) percent decrease never lands you back at the start, since \(\left(1 + \tfrac{p}{100}\right)\left(1 - \tfrac{p}{100}\right)\) is always a hair under \(1\), leaving you a touch below where you began. The same multiplying is what drives steady growth, rising \(10\%\) each period just means multiplying by \(1.10\) over and over.
